Post_Text: Your staff member showed their insurance card like they were supposed to. The plan paid more than the cash price posted down the street. That happens on nearly half of shoppable services. Here's the data nobody on your account is reading: [LINK]

Same MRI. Same machine. Same read. The price swings more than tenfold inside one hospital and more than thirtyfold across a state. Based mostly on who is paying. This is why your plan's biggest line is also its most rigged: [LINK]

Your staff member handed over the insurance card and the plan paid more than the cash price. That happens about half the time, and nobody on your account is checking. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)

You assume the expensive facility is the better one. The link between price and quality in healthcare is weak, and often just not there. A higher price tag buys location and leverage, not a better surgeon. Read why before your next renewal: [LINK]

Before you renew the same network, see how its 'discount' actually stacks up against the published cash price. On the high-volume services, the gap is often the wrong direction. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)

Post_Text: Your network "negotiated a 50% discount." Off what number, exactly? The chargemaster, a list price set high on purpose. Half of a rigged number is still a rigged number. Here's the mechanic the plan is built to keep quiet: [LINK]

A Health Affairs study checked 70 services across 2,000+ hospitals. In roughly 47% of cases, the cash price was at or below the price your insurance negotiated. The card is the more expensive option close to half the time. See the numbers: [LINK]

Every hospital in the country has been required to publish its prices since January 2021. Cash price, negotiated rate, all of it, in a public file. Most school plans have never had anyone open one. Here's what's sitting in there: [LINK]

One question for whoever handles your school's health plan: On the procedures your staff use most, has anyone ever set the published cash price next to what your plan actually paid? If no one has, how would you know which way the gap runs? [LINK]
